questions.question: Explain the three fundamental binary tree traversal methods with their visit orders.
keyPoints: Inorder (LNR): Left subtree → Root → Right subtree
Preorder (NLR): Root → Left subtree → Right subtree
Postorder (LRN): Left subtree → Right subtree → Root
Level Order: Level by level from top to bottom

questions.question: Write an algorithm for recursive Inorder traversal of a binary tree.
keyPoints: Base case: if node is NULL, return
Recursively traverse left subtree
Visit the root node (process data)
Recursively traverse right subtree
Time: O(n), Space: O(h) where h is height

questions.question: Why is Level Order traversal implemented using a queue instead of a stack?
keyPoints: Queue ensures FIFO (First In First Out) order
This maintains level-by-level processing
When node is processed, children are enqueued
Children wait while all nodes at current level are processed
Stack would give depth-first instead of breadth-first

questions.question: What are the applications of Preorder and Postorder traversals?
keyPoints: Preorder: Create tree copy, serialize tree, prefix expressions
Postorder: Delete tree nodes (free children before parent), postfix expressions
Postorder also used for evaluating expression trees
Useful for directory structure calculations

questions.question: Analyze the time and space complexity of tree traversal algorithms.
keyPoints: Time complexity: O(n) - each node visited exactly once
Space complexity: O(h) where h is height of tree
Worst case (skewed tree): O(n)
Best case (balanced tree): O(log n)
Morris traversal achieves O(1) auxiliary space

questions.question: What is Morris Inorder Traversal and why is it significant?
keyPoints: Morris traversal traverses without recursion or stack
Uses threaded links (temporary) to find inorder predecessor
Creates and removes links during traversal
Achieves O(1) auxiliary space
Significant for memory-constrained environments

questions.question: How can a binary tree be uniquely reconstructed from traversal sequences?
keyPoints: Any two traversals with inorder are sufficient
Preorder + Inorder: First element of preorder is root, split inorder
Postorder + Inorder: Last element of postorder is root, split inorder
Inorder alone cannot uniquely identify the tree structure
Tree structure depends on relative positions of nodes

questions.question: Write an algorithm for iterative Inorder traversal of a binary tree using a stack. Explain the procedure with an example tree.
keyPoints: Initialize stack and current pointer to root
Push all left children until reaching NULL
Pop from stack, process node, move to right
Repeat until stack empty and current is NULL
Trace with example: root with left and right subtrees
Show step-by-step visualization of stack states
Code should handle empty tree and all edge cases

questions.question: Write and explain the algorithm for Level Order traversal of a binary tree using a queue. Also discuss its applications.
keyPoints: Initialize queue with root node
While queue not empty: dequeue node, process it, enqueue its children
Continue until all levels processed
Time: O(n), Space: O(max nodes at any level)
Applications: Finding shortest path in unweighted tree
Level order also used in: tree width calculation, level-wise printing
Can be modified to print level markers, find level of each node

questions.question: Compare Depth First Search (DFS) and Breadth First Search (BFS) traversals in binary trees. How are they implemented and when is each preferred?
keyPoints: DFS: Inorder, Preorder, Postorder - uses stack (explicit or implicit)
BFS: Level Order - uses queue
DFS explores deep before backtracking, BFS explores level by level
DFS: O(h) space, BFS: O(w) space where w is max width
DFS preferred for: path finding, cycle detection, topological sort
BFS preferred for: shortest path, level-wise operations, finding nearest nodes
Trade-offs: DFS may cause stack overflow in deep trees, BFS requires more memory for wide trees

questions.question: For a binary search tree, explain why Inorder traversal produces sorted output. Also write an algorithm to find the kth smallest element using inorder traversal.
keyPoints: BST property: left subtree < root < right subtree
Inorder visits left (all smaller), then root, then right (all larger)
Therefore nodes are visited in ascending order
To find kth smallest: perform inorder, maintain counter
Stop when counter equals k
Time: O(k + h) for balanced tree, O(n) worst case
Can optimize with augmented BST storing subtree sizes
